turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court in case 21-1449, Glacier Northwest v. the International Brotherhood of Teamsters.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: The parties to this case are Glacier Northwest, a company that delivers ready mix concrete to customers in Washington State and the International Brotherhood of Teamsters Local Union No. 174, which serves as the bargaining representative for Glacier's truck drivers. After relations between Glacier and the Union soured, the truck drivers went on strike. They did so when Glacier was in the middle of creating and delivering a large amount of wet concrete. After the dust settled, Glacier sued the Union for damages in Washington State Court alleging that the Union had intentionally destroyed the company's concrete by executing the strike the way that it did. Now, our case law permits a party to stop a State Court proceeding by showing that the National Labor Relations Act, which I'll call the NLRA for short, arguably conflicts with relevant state law. The Union tried to do that here. It moved to dismiss the case on the ground that Glacier's state tort claims are preempted by the NLRA because the strike was arguably protected by Federal Law. Today's case evaluates that argument which the Washington Supreme Court accepted. We hold that the NLRA does not preempt Glacier State Law claims. All agree that the NLRA protects the right of workers to go on strike but I'll also agree that this right is not absolute and the parties agree that one limit on the right to strike is the principle that the workers must take reasonable precautions to protect their employer's property from foreseeable and imminent danger due to the workers' decision to stop work. Accepting the allegations and Glacier's complaint as true, we conclude that the Union has not carried its burden of establishing that the NLRA arguably protects the drivers' conduct. Glacier alleges the drivers' conduct created an emergency in which it had to come up with a way to offload wet concrete from its delivery trucks in a timely manner so as to avoid costly damage to the trucks. The Union's actions not only resulted in the destruction of all the concrete Glacier had prepared that day, they also posed a risk of foreseeable and imminent danger to the trucks themselves. Because the Union took affirmative steps to endanger Glacier's property rather than reasonable precautions to mitigate that risk, the NLRA does not arguably protect its conduct. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Washington Supreme Court and remand the case for further proceedings not inconsistent with our opinion. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, which Justice Gorsuch joins. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in which Justices Thomas and Gorsuch join. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion.
